variables.content:
For a two-dimensional, noninteracting electron gas with effective mass m^* and carrier density N the zero-temperature Fermi energy is E_F = (pi ħ^2 N) / m^*, so a small density change delta N produces a chemical-potential change delta mu_F = (pi ħ^2 / m^*) delta N. Using the GaAs effective electron mass m^* = 0.067 m_e (with m_e the free-electron rest mass) and substituting delta N = 0.7 × 10^14 m^{-2} gives delta mu_F ≈ 0.22 meV; the proposition is that this 2D free-electron relation with m^* = 0.067 m_e is applicable as an estimate for the high-density quantum-well HM2DEG in the studied parameter range.
      Standard 2D electron gas relations; Jenichen et al., Section III.B

The Fermi temperature of the two-dimensional electron gas is calculated from the sheet electron density $n_s$ according to $T_F=\hbar^2\pi n_s/(k_B m^*)$ using the effective mass $m^*=0.19\,m_e$, where $m_e$ is the free-electron mass, and the analysis assumes that this effective mass remains applicable in the dilute low-density regime near the metal-insulator transition.

For the ideal (strictly two-dimensional, zero-width, disorder-free) paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass remains close to unity across the studied density parameters r_s=1, 5, and 10, and the high-precision DMC extrapolations reported here show no evidence for a strong enhancement of m^* at low density in this ideal system.

For a quasi-two-dimensional electron gas occupying a single electronic sub-band with twofold spin degeneracy and with isotropic parabolic dispersion characterized by an effective electron mass $m^{*}$ (where $m^{*}$ denotes the effective mass and $m_{0}$ denotes the free-electron mass), the two-dimensional electron density per unit area $n_{2D}$ and the Fermi energy $E_{f}$ measured above the sub-band bottom are related by the quasi-two-dimensional density-of-states relation
      $E_{f} = \dfrac{\pi \hbar^{2} n_{2D}}{m^{*}}$,
      where $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant. The simplifying modeling proposition asserted for data reduction is: adopting a single constant averaged effective mass $m^{*}=0.028\,m_{0}$ across the experimental energy range is treated as an approximation that accounts for nonparabolicity sufficiently well to permit a reliable conversion from an experimentally determined $n_{2D}$ (from SdH analysis) to the Fermi energy $E_{f}$ by the formula above; in this approximation $m^{*}$ is taken as energy-independent.

The Fermi energy E_F of a two-dimensional electron gas with sheet density n and effective mass m* is given, under the parabolic-dispersion approximation with energy-independent effective mass, by E_F = π ℏ^2 n / m* (measured from the subband bottom); substituting n = 2.85 × 10^11 cm^-2 and m* = 0.10 m_0 yields E_F = 6.75 meV.

For semiclassical estimates of the Fermi velocity in the two-dimensional hole gas we take an effective hole mass $m^{*}\approx0.10\,m_{e}$ (where $m_{e}$ is the electron rest mass) and compute the Fermi velocity as $v_{f}=h k_{f}/m^{*}$ with $k_{f}=\sqrt{2\pi\rho_{2D}}$ for a two-dimensional carrier density $\rho_{2D}$; this proposition assumes a single parabolic band with $m^{*}\approx0.10\,m_{e}$ adequately describes the low-energy dispersion of the hole gas for the carrier-density range of interest.

Treating the near-surface accumulated electrons as a two-dimensional (2D) free-electron gas with in-plane effective mass m* = 0.023 m0 (m0 denotes the free-electron mass, m0 ≈ 9.109×10^-31 kg) and with the Fermi energy E_F located 0.2 eV above the bulk conduction-band minimum (CBM), the sheet electron density n_s computed from the 2D free-electron relation n_s = m* E_F/(π ħ^2) is approximately 10^12 cm^-2.

Define the single-particle density of states for an ideal two-dimensional electron gas as D_0 = 2m/(πħ^2), where m is the electron effective mass and ħ is the reduced Planck constant. The proposition is that the effective mass m entering D_0 can be taken to have the value m = 0.19 m_e (with m_e the free electron mass) independently of the application of a strong in-plane magnetic field sufficient to fully polarize spins (B > B_sat), so that comparisons of the measured D* to multiples of D_0 using m = 0.19 m_e remain valid in both unpolarized and fully spin-polarized regimes; this assumes that interaction and polarization effects do not significantly renormalize m under the experimental conditions.

For a two-dimensional electron system the dimensionless interaction parameter r_s (the ratio of a characteristic Coulomb energy to the Fermi energy) scales (up to order-unity geometry-dependent prefactors) as r_s ∝ m^*/(ε √{n_{2D}}), where m^* is the effective electron mass, ε is the effective dielectric constant, and n_{2D} is the two-dimensional carrier density; using m^* ≈ 0.5 m_e (m_e the free-electron mass), ε ≈ 8 for multilayer MoS2, and n_{2D} ≈ 3×10^{12} cm^{-2} yields an r_s of order 9, which indicates strong electron–electron Coulomb interactions in the explored density range, subject to the caveats that this estimate neglects band-structure-specific prefactors, screening from proximate dielectric layers or gates beyond the effective ε, and many-body renormalizations of m^* that could modify the numerical value.

Applying the Lundqvist–Overhauser single-mode self-energy model for Σ(k,E) together with the exact effective-mass relation obtained by differentiating the Dyson equation (m*/m given above) in the strictly two-dimensional inversion-layer limit (zero transverse thickness), the computed quasiparticle dispersion E(k) remains nearly parabolic and yields a mean effective mass m* about 14% larger than the bulk in-plane band mass m at two-dimensional carrier density N = 10^12 cm^{-2}; the calculated m* decreases as N increases over the range 10^11–10^13 cm^{-2}. When these zero-magnetic-field theoretical masses are compared to magnetoconductivity measurements reported by Smith and Stiles, the theoretical effective-mass curve lies approximately 0.005 m_e (m_e denotes the free-electron mass) below the lower-field experimental points over the experimental density range.
